does: This paper introduces Adaptive Instruction Composition, a framework for automated red-teaming of large language models that strategically combines crowdsourced harmful queries and tactics into attack instructions. The core innovation involves training a reinforcement learning mechanism to optimize the selection and combination of instruction components, balancing effectiveness (successful jailbreaks) against diversity (varied attack strategies). The RL agent navigates a combinatorial space of possible instruction combinations to adaptively guide an attacker LLM toward generating diverse adversarial prompts tailored to specific target model vulnerabilities. The approach substantially outperforms random combination baselines across multiple effectiveness and diversity metrics, maintains performance under model transfer scenarios, and achieves superior results compared to recent adaptive red-teaming methods when evaluated on the Harmbench benchmark.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of LLM red-teaming, which has traditionally relied on either brute-force trial-and-error approaches where attacker models discover jailbreaks independently, or simpler composition methods that randomly combine crowdsourced harmful components without principled selection. The paper extends prior work on diversity-aware adversarial attack generation and draws connections to compositional attack strategies explored in cybersecurity and adversarial ML. It responds to limitations in existing adaptive approaches by framing the problem as a structured optimization challenge rather than unguided search, incorporating insights from combinatorial optimization and reinforcement learning for exploration-exploitation tradeoffs. The work also engages with recent benchmarking efforts like Harmbench that systematize red-teaming evaluation.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more efficient and comprehensive red-teaming by automating the discovery of diverse, high-impact jailbreaks without exhaustive human effort. The adaptive composition mechanism could generalize beyond LLMs to other safety-critical systems requiring adversarial testing, and the RL-guided approach offers a blueprint for other domains combining discrete components under optimization constraints. Future work could extend the framework to learn which crowdsourced components are most valuable for specific target architectures, enable real-time adaptation based on emerging model variants, or integrate human feedback loops to steer the discovered attacks toward policy-relevant vulnerabilities. The methodology may also inform defensive strategies by revealing systematic patterns in how diverse attacks are constructed, ultimately supporting the development of more robust LLM alignment and safety mechanisms.
